name: Iris
description: Iris, in Greek mythology, is the personification of the rainbow and a messenger of the gods. She is often depicted as a beautiful young woman with golden wings, carrying a herald's staff or caduceus. As a messenger, Iris primarily serves Hera, Queen of the Gods, although she also carries messages for Zeus. Her swiftness and reliability are emphasized in mythological accounts, enabling her to traverse great distances between Olympus and the mortal realm, as well as the Underworld. Iris is not only a messenger but also acts as a divine handmaiden, attending to Hera's needs and performing various tasks such as fetching water from the River Styx to swear oaths. Her association with the rainbow links her to communication, new endeavors, and promises. While Hermes is more commonly associated with messages, Iris holds a distinct role, often conveying messages that require grace and speed, reflecting her connection to the heavens and divine authority. She is also associated with the sea and is sometimes described as a sea goddess.
subtitle: Goddess of the Rainbow

keyMyths.title: Iris and the Golden Apples
keyMyths.description: In one account, Iris is tasked with retrieving water from the River Styx to revive the dying hero, Heracles. She flies to the Underworld, braving the dangers of the realm of Hades, to collect the water, which possesses restorative powers. This highlights her courage and dedication to her duties as a messenger and servant of the gods. Additionally, Iris is often depicted as carrying golden apples, symbolizing divine favor and immortality, which she delivers to mortals or other deities as gifts from the gods.

keyMyths.title: Iris as Messenger to Alcyone
keyMyths.description: The myth of Alcyone and Ceyx recounts how Iris, at the behest of Hera, is sent to Alcyone in a dream to inform her of her husband Ceyx's death at sea. Iris travels to the land of Somnus, the god of sleep, and instructs him to send a dream-image of Ceyx to Alcyone. This myth showcases Iris's role in delivering both joyous and sorrowful messages, highlighting the full spectrum of human experience that she mediates between the gods and mortals.

keyMyths.title: Iris and the Harpies
keyMyths.description: In some versions of the Argonautica, Iris intervenes to prevent the Argonauts from killing the Harpies, monstrous creatures who were tormenting the blind king Phineus. Iris promises that the Harpies will no longer bother Phineus, saving them from the wrath of the Argonauts. This demonstrates her role as a mediator and a force for maintaining order and balance in the world, even when dealing with creatures considered monstrous or dangerous.
